mnemonics.title: Oh Omega Theta
mnemonics.content: Big O (O) = Upper Bound (Worst Case) → 'O' stands for 'On top' or 'Over' (maximum). Big Omega (Ω) = Lower Bound (Best Case) → 'Ω' looks like an 'U' for 'Under' (minimum). Big Theta (Θ) = Tight Bound → 'Theta' sounds like 'The Exact'

mnemonics.title: FAST Log Loop
mnemonics.content: For Recurrence Relations: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). Remember: 'F' = number of subproblems (a), 'A' = divisor (b), 'S' = split cost, 'T' = time. Compare n^(logb a) with f(n) to find complexity.

mnemonics.title: 1 Log Linear Quad Cubic Exponential
mnemonics.content: Order of common complexities from fastest to slowest: O(1) → O(log n) → O(n) → O(n log n) → O(n²) → O(n³) → O(2^n) → O(n!). First letters: 1 L L Q C E F (1 for constant, L for logarithmic, etc.)

mnemonics.title: Best Average Worst
mnemonics.content: Best Case (Ω) = 'Best' possible input gives this. Average Case (Θ) = Expected running time. Worst Case (O) = 'Worst' input causes maximum work. Remember: B-A-W order matches Ω-Θ-O.

mnemonics.title: Drop the Constants
mnemonics.content: When analyzing: 3n² + 2n + 1 → Drop coefficients (3, 2, 1) and lower order terms → O(n²). Because 'n² dominates n dominates constant' as n grows.

acronyms.expansion: Time function of input size n
acronyms.usage: Used to express exact running time of an algorithm as a function of input size n
Used when we want to say 'the algorithm will NOT take more than this time.' Worst case analysis.
Used when we want to say 'the algorithm will take AT LEAST this time.' Best case analysis.
Used when upper and lower bounds are the same. The algorithm's exact growth rate.
f(n) = o(g(n)) means f grows strictly slower than g. f(n)/g(n) → 0 as n→∞
f(n) = ω(g(n)) means f grows strictly faster than g. g(n)/f(n) → 0 as n→∞

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a graph with n on X-axis and time on Y-axis. Draw curves: flat horizontal line = O(1), very shallow curve = O(log n), straight 45° line = O(n), parabola = O(n²), steep exponential = O(2^n). The steeper the curve, the worse the complexity.
Visualize boxes within boxes. One loop = 1D line (n). Two nested loops = 2D square grid (n²). Three nested loops = 3D cube (n³). Count how many loops are nested to determine polynomial degree.
For O(log n), picture a balanced binary tree. Each level halves the problem. Depth of tree with n nodes = log₂ n. Cutting the tree in half repeatedly until one node remains.

Draw each recursive call as a node. Branching factor = number of recursive calls. Sum all levels: leaves at bottom show total work. T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n becomes a tree with branching factor 2 and n work per level.
Compare f(n) with n^(logb a). Three cases: (1) f(n) smaller → answer is n^(logb a), (2) f(n) same → answer is n^(logb a) × log n, (3) f(n) larger → answer is f(n) if polynomial difference.

analogies.analogy: Looking up a specific word in a dictionary by directly opening to that page (knowing the page number). No matter how thick the dictionary, one action gets the result.
Finding a word in a dictionary by opening to the middle, checking if word is before/after, then repeating on half. Each step cuts work in half. For 1000 pages, only ~10 steps needed.
Reading every page of a book to find a specific word. If the book has n pages, you might need to read all n pages in worst case.
Sorting a deck of cards: look at each card (n), and for each card, find its correct position among already sorted cards (log n on average). Like merge sort or quick sort.
Comparing every student with every other student in a class to find who is taller. With n students, you need n × n comparisons. Classic nested loops.
Trying all possible combinations of a password. If password has n characters from 26 letters, you have 26^n possibilities. Adding one character doubles the work!
Listing all possible seating arrangements for n people at a round table. Person 1 has n choices, person 2 has (n-1), and so on → n × (n-1) × (n-2) × ... × 1 = n!
Imagine a parking lot. If you need only 1 spot regardless of how many cars arrive = O(1) space. If you need to store every arriving car = O(n) space. Memory is like parking spaces for your data.
